mnemonics.title: Architecture Decision Factors
mnemonics.content: SCALE: Scalability, Cost, Availability, Latency, Extensibility - key factors to consider when making architecture decisions

mnemonics.title: Three-Tier Layers
mnemonics.content: PAD: Presentation (user interface), Application (business logic), Data (storage) - remember the PAD for understanding 3-tier architecture

mnemonics.title: CAP Theorem
mnemonics.content: CAP: Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance - Can Always Pick Two, hence CAP

acronyms.usage: Maximum acceptable downtime after a disaster - defines how quickly services must be restored
Maximum acceptable data loss measured in time - defines how much data can be lost before severe impact
Measures system reliability - higher MTBF indicates more reliable systems
Average time to restore a failed system - lower MTTR means faster recovery
Theorem stating distributed systems can only guarantee 2 of 3 properties
Complete cost including acquisition, operation, maintenance, and disposal
Contract defining expected service standards and performance metrics

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a restaurant: Presentation is the waiter (takes orders), Application is the kitchen (prepares food), Data is the storage/pantry (ingredients)
Vertical = building a taller building on same land; Horizontal = building more buildings on more land
Imagine multiple cash counters at a supermarket - customers are distributed across counters to avoid long queues at one
Think of a triangle - you can only sit on two corners, can't touch all three. Same with CAP properties in distributed systems
Think of LEGO blocks - each small piece serves a purpose and can be added/removed/replaced independently

analogies.analogy: Like a restaurant during peak hours - scaling up is adding more chefs to existing kitchen, scaling out is opening more restaurant branches
Renting vs buying a house - cloud is renting (flexible, pay monthly), on-premise is buying (expensive upfront but own the property)
A traffic signal at intersections - distributes cars (requests) to prevent congestion at any single point
A bridge on a single road - if it breaks, all traffic stops; architecture needs multiple bridges (redundancy)
A hospital with specialized departments (cardiology, neurology) - each handles specific functions but work together for patient care
